perturbation.original_text: 1.1. Affiliate shall mean, when used with reference to a party, any individual or entity directly or indirectly controlling, controlled by or under common control with such party. For purposes of this definition, "control" means the direct or indirect ownership of at least 50% of the outstanding voting securities of a party, or the right to control the policy decisions of such party.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1. Affiliate shall mean, when used with reference to a party, any individual or entity directly or indirectly controlling, controlled by or under common control with such party. For purposes of this definition, "control" means the direct or indirect ownership of at least 25% of the outstanding voting securities of a party, or the right to control the policy decisions of such party.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the percentage of ownership that defines 'control' from 'at least 50%' to 'at least 25%', this introduces a contradiction. This is because later sections, such as Section 13.2, which refers to 'Affiliate' in the context of assigning the agreement without consent, now become ambiguous. It's unclear if transferring to an entity controlled with 25% ownership bypasses the consent requirement, while another entity might consider 50% to be the requirement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 13.2. No Assignment. Except as otherwise set forth herein, neither party shall transfer, assign or cede any rights or delegate any obligations hereunder, in whole or in part, whether voluntarily or by operation of law, without the prior written consent of the other party, which consent may be withheld at the other party's reasonable business discretion; provided, however, that either party may transfer this Agreement without prior written consent of the other party to an Affiliate or in connection with a merger or sale of all or substantially all of the stock or assets of such party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'control' for the purpose of defining 'Affiliate' from 50% to 25% ownership. This directly contradicts Section 13.2, which allows assignment to an Affiliate without prior written consent. With the altered definition, it's now unclear whether a transfer to an entity controlled with 25% ownership would bypass the consent requirement, while previously it was clear that only 50% or more constituted control. This ambiguity creates a significant in-text contradiction regarding the conditions under which assignment is permitted without consent, impacting the enforceability of the assignment clause.

perturbation.original_text: 1.22. PaperExchange Revenue shall mean the gross revenue received by PaperExchange from Transaction Fees less (a) credits for claims, allowances, retroactive price reductions or returned goods, and (b) sales, excise, use, value-added and other similar taxes (excluding income taxes) actually paid.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.22. PaperExchange Revenue shall mean the gross revenue received by PaperExchange from Transaction Fees less (a) credits for claims, allowances, retroactive price reductions or returned goods, (b) sales, excise, use, value-added and other similar taxes (excluding income taxes) actually paid, and (c) marketing expenses.
perturbation.explanation: By adding '(c) marketing expenses' to the definition of 'PaperExchange Revenue', this creates a conflict with Section 7.4, which dictates revenue sharing with VerticalNet based on PaperExchange Revenue. The definition now allows PaperExchange to reduce the revenue subject to sharing by deducting marketing expenses, while Section 7.4's sharing calculation is based on the original definition.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7.4. Revenue Sharing. After PaperExchange has generated PaperExchange Revenue equal to *****, PaperExchange shall pay an amount equal to ***** of the PaperExchange Revenue to VerticalNet; provided, however, that if, in any given calendar year, VerticalNet receives ***** pursuant to this Section 7.4 [Revenue Sharing], the percentage of PaperExchange Revenue that PaperExchange shall pay to VerticalNet for the remainder of such calendar year shall be reduced to *****; and provided further, however, that if, in any given calendar year, VerticalNet receives * Confidential Treatment Requested: material has been omitted and filed separately with the Commission. 12 ***** in the aggregate pursuant to this Section 7.4 [Revenue Sharing], the percentage of PaperExchange Revenue that PaperExchange shall pay to VerticalNet for the remainder of such calendar year shall be reduced to *****.
perturbation.justification: The addition of '(c) marketing expenses' to the definition of 'PaperExchange Revenue' directly contradicts Section 7.4, which outlines revenue sharing with VerticalNet based on PaperExchange Revenue. The revised definition allows PaperExchange to reduce the revenue subject to sharing by deducting marketing expenses, altering the base for the percentage calculation defined in Section 7.4. This is a clear and impactful conflict because it changes the amount of revenue available for sharing as originally agreed, potentially leading to disputes about the amounts owed under the revenue-sharing arrangement.

perturbation.original_text: 13.5. Notices. All notices, reports, payments and other communications required or permitted to be given under this Agreement (each, a "Notice") shall be in writing and shall be given either by personal delivery against a signed receipt, by express delivery using a nationally recognized overnight courier, or by facsimile.
perturbation.changed_text: 13.5. Notices. All notices, reports, payments and other communications required or permitted to be given under this Agreement (each, a "Notice") shall be in writing and may be given via email, personal delivery against a signed receipt, by express delivery using a nationally recognized overnight courier, or by facsimile.
perturbation.explanation: By including 'email' to the means of giving notice within this contract, it will create an in-text contradiction as it has not being expressed on earlier sections where it talks about notices. For example, it creates contradiction on Section 8.2, regarding 'Termination for Cause', which specifies 'written notice' without accounting for email. Now there is contradiction in what is being stated in the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8.2. Termination for Cause. Either party may terminate this Agreement immediately upon written notice to the other party in the event any material breach of a material term of this Agreement by such other party that remains uncured 30 days in the case of a breach of a payment obligation, or 45 days for all other breaches, after notice of such breach was received by such other party; provided, however that if such breach is not reasonably capable of cure within the applicable cure period, the breaching party shall have an additional 180 days to cure such breach so long as the cure is commenced within the applicable cure period and thereafter is diligently prosecuted to completion as soon as possible.
perturbation.justification: The addition of 'email' as a permissible method of notice in Section 13.5 directly contradicts the requirement of 'written notice' in Section 8.2. The original contract language in 13.5 specified methods like personal delivery or express courier, implying a level of formality and verifiability absent in email. Now, with email included in 13.5, it is unclear if an email constitutes sufficient 'written notice' under Section 8.2, creating a strong in-text contradiction regarding the proper procedure for terminating the agreement for cause. This ambiguity could lead to disputes over the validity of termination notices served via email.
